coredata.dc:title: A novel type of carbon dots for discriminating yeast cell viability and nuclear labeling
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Long
Xie, Zhixin
Cao, Ming
Qin, Shuaifan
Chen, Yongshi
Tian, Bo
Feng, Zhibiao
coredata.dc:description: As a new type of nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have demonstrated potential in the field of biological imaging. Currently, research utilizing them for labeling microorganisms is confined to simple imaging, with limited studies exploring their application in detecting cell viability and organelle targeting. Yeast, serving as both a fermenting agent and a model cell, constitutes an ideal research subject This study synthesized ascorbic acid-based CDs (A-CDs) using citric acid, ascorbic acid, and ethylenediamine as raw materials via hydrothermal method. A-CDs were uniformly dispersed, with an average particle size of 3.28 nm. The surface functional groups, such as NH2 and -OH, endowed them with hydrophilicity. Additionally, A-CDs exhibited concentration-dependence, good photostability, and biocompatibility. By labeling yeast with A-CDs, it was observed that cells in dead, alive, and intermediate states exhibited different imaging effects, which were highly correlated with the concentration of A-CDs entering the cell due to variations in cell membrane permeability. Through co-localization experiments with A-CDs and DAPI, it was confirmed that A-CDs could target and label cell nucleus. Preliminary endocytosis experiments indicated that A-CDs primarily entered cells via clathrin-mediated and lipid raft-mediated endocytosis. The ability of A-CDs to discriminate between cell viability and label nuclei offers new perspectives on the application of CDs in the field of biological imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Viability
Carbon dots
Ascorbic acid
Nuclear labeling

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province ( LH2019C020 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of A-CDs. (A) TEM image and particle size distribution. (B) High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image. (C) XRD pattern. (D) Raman spectrum. (E) FTIR spectrum. (F) XPS full spectrum. (G) C1s spectrum. (H) N1s spectrum. (I) O1s spectrum.

Optical properties of A-CDs. (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence emission spectrum (λem = 460 nm), and fluorescence excitation spectrum (λex = 344 nm). (B) Fluorescence spectra of A-CDs (λex = 320–400 nm). (C) Fluorescence spectra of A-CDs with different concentrations. (D–F) The effects of pH, PBS ion concentration and UV on the fluorescence intensity of A-CDs, respectively.
Toxicity of A-CDs. (A) Growth curves of yeast co-incubated with different concentrations of A-CDs. (B) Colonies of yeast co-incubated with different concentrations of A-CDs.

Yeast cell imaging analysis. (A) Yeast cells stained with A-CDs. (B) Yeast cells stained with N-CDs. (C) Yeast cells treated with ethanol and stained with A-CDs. (D) Yeast cells stained with A-CDs (a) Bright field image (b–e) Yeast cells stained with A-CDs captured at different time points (UV excitation).
Nuclear targeted analysis of A-CDs. (A) Yeast cells stained with A-CDs. (B) Yeast cells co-stained with A-CDs and DAPI.
Uptake pathways of A-CDs. Yeast cells stained with A-CDs after different treatments: (A) 28 °C (control) (B) 4 °C. (C) 2-DG. (D) CPZ. (E) GEN. (F) MβCD.

head.ce:title: A novel type of carbon dots for discriminating yeast cell viability and nuclear labeling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, 150030, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of food science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, 150030, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new type of nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have demonstrated potential in the field of biological imaging. Currently, research utilizing them for labeling microorganisms is confined to simple imaging, with limited studies exploring their application in detecting cell viability and organelle targeting. Yeast, serving as both a fermenting agent and a model cell, constitutes an ideal research subject This study synthesized ascorbic acid-based CDs (A-CDs) using citric acid, ascorbic acid, and ethylenediamine as raw materials via hydrothermal method. A-CDs were uniformly dispersed, with an average particle size of 3.28 nm. The surface functional groups, such as NH2 and -OH, endowed them with hydrophilicity. Additionally, A-CDs exhibited concentration-dependence, good photostability, and biocompatibility. By labeling yeast with A-CDs, it was observed that cells in dead, alive, and intermediate states exhibited different imaging effects, which were highly correlated with the concentration of A-CDs entering the cell due to variations in cell membrane permeability. Through co-localization experiments with A-CDs and DAPI, it was confirmed that A-CDs could target and label cell nucleus. Preliminary endocytosis experiments indicated that A-CDs primarily entered cells via clathrin-mediated and lipid raft-mediated endocytosis. The ability of A-CDs to discriminate between cell viability and label nuclei offers new perspectives on the application of CDs in the field of biological imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel type of zero-dimensional material with a size of less than 10 nm in diameter (Chen et al., 2024a,b,c; Chen et al., 2024a,b,c), also known as carbon nanodots or carbon nanoparticles (Shan et al., 2024). Compared to traditional fluorescent dyes, CDs offer the advantage of enhanced biocompatibility, fluorescence stability, and tunable fluorescence, and have been widely used in fields such as biological imaging, fluorescence sensing (Bian et al., 2025; Lin et al., 2023; Rajalakshmi et al., 2024), and photoelectrocatalysis (Achem et al., 2024; Pakapongpan et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2023; Zdražil et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2016). Especially in the field of biological imaging, CDs are one of the most competitive materials (Chen et al., 2024a,b,c; Dai et al., 2022; Li et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020; Oliveira et al., 2023; Song et al., 2019a,b; Wang & Lu, 2022; Zhang et al., 2025).

The preparation method, carbon source, and doped heteroatoms are the main factors affecting the function of CDs. Among various preparation methods, hydrothermal method is widely used due to its simple operation, low equipment requirements, and low energy consumption. Citric acid (CA) has become a popular carbon source due to its ability to provide hydroxyl groups and form stable carbon nucleus (Wysocka et al., 2024). Doping with heteroatoms has become a robust and universal method to improve the fluorescence performance of CDs (Feng et al., 2021). Nitrogen is often doped because the dehydration condensation reaction between the amino group in the nitrogen source and the carboxyl group in the carbon source leads to the formation of cross-linked polymers and the embedding of various functional groups into CDs (Li et al., 2015; Pakapongpan et al., 2024; Si et al., 2021). Ethylenediamine (EDA), as a representative substance of N-doping, is widely used due to its ability to modulate energy levels, band gaps, optical properties, and fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs (Wysocka et al., 2024). Ascorbic acid (AA) is a trace substance required for biological life activities (Magar et al., 2024), with good biocompatibility, abundant hydroxyl groups, and strong reducibility (Kochetkova et al., 2025). It can endow CDs with more functional characteristics through redox reactions (Pota et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2024).

In the field of biological imaging, good biocompatibility and excellent fluorescence properties of CDs are necessary, and endowing CDs with special functions is still a challenge. Zhu et al. synthesized CDs using CA as carbon source, EDA as nitrogen source, and water as solvent. Although the CDs had ultra-high QY (Zhu et al., 2015), like most CDs, they did not exhibit specific labeling function in biological imaging. CDs with high QY and targeting function has broad application prospects in the field of biological imaging (Peng et al., 2017). Sun et al. synthesized CDs targeting endoplasmic reticulum, lysosome, and nucleus using tartaric acid and phenylenediamine as raw materials (Sun et al., 2024). Song et al. endowed CDs organelle-targeting function by crosslinking nucleus-targeting peptides with CDs (Song et al., 2019a,b). Yang et al. physically modified iRGD (tumor penetrating peptide) onto CDs to achieve targeted effects on tumor cells (Yang et al., 2018). Xing et al. prepared CDs with endoplasmic reticulum targeting ability by adjusting the ratio of tridecanedioic acid to m-diethylaminophenol (Xing et al., 2024). So far, research on CDs labeling organelles has generally been limited to animal cells and rarely involved microorganisms. Yeast, as a typical representative of fungal microorganisms, has a wide range of applications in the food industry and bioscience research (He et al., 2024; Heppner & Livney, 2025), so it is of great significance to develop CDs capable of determining the active state and organelle-targeting labeling ability of yeast.

In this study, AA, EDA, and CA were used as raw materials to prepare A-CDs via one-step hydrothermal method, and their structure and optical properties were investigated. We used A-CDs to image yeast, analyze the survival status of yeast, determine the function of A-CDs targeting the nucleus, and explore the uptake pathway of A-CDs, aiming to provide a novel CDs and expand their application in the field of biological imaging.

EDA was purchased from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Anhydrous ethanol, phosphoric acid and CA were purchased from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). AA was purchased from Beijing Boao Tuoda Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Acetic acid was purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Sodium hydroxide was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Science and Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Chlorpromazine (CPZ) and quinine sulfate were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Yeast (High activity) was purchased from Angel Yeast Co., Ltd. (Yichang, China). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution was purchased from Beijing Labgic Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The fluorescent dye 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) was purchased from Beijing Solarbio Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Yeast extract peptone dextrose medium (YEPD) was purchased from Qingdao Hopel Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China). Genistein (GEN) and 2-deoxy-D-glucose (2-DG) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methyl β-cyclodextrin (MβCD) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

CA (1.5 g), AA (0.7 g) and EDA (1 mL) were added to 20 mL of distilled water. The mixture was stirred thoroughly at room temperature for 5 min, then transferred to a reaction kettle lined with PTFE, and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After complete cooling, the obtained mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous filtration membrane, and then dialyzed against distilled water in a dialysis bag (500 D) for 24 h. The solution in the dialysis bag was freeze-dried to obtain a solid powder, denoted as A-CDs. In addition, N-CDs synthesized from CA and EDA via the hydrothermal method (Zhang et al., 2023) were used as controls for fluorescence imaging analysis of yeast.

The morphology of A-CDs was characterized using a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, Japan). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra of A-CDs were recorded using a UH5300 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence spectra of A-CDs were recorded using an F-4700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). FTIR-650 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Zhongke, China) was used to obtain the FT-IR spectra of A-CDs. The elemental composition of A-CDs was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The crystal structures of A-CDs were analyzed using a Raman spectrometer (Thin Via, Renishaw, UK) and an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany).

The fluorescence QY is an important parameter to evaluate the luminescence performance of CDs. Quinine sulfate with a QY of 0.54 in water was used as a standard for calculating the QY of A-CDs. Referring to the formula in the literature of Mehta et al. (Mehta et al., 2014).
(1)
Q Y
( x )
=
QY R
×
I x
/
I R
×
A R
/
A x
×
n x 2
/
n R 2

In this formula, the subscripts x and R represent the test sample and reference, respectively, where I corresponds to fluorescence intensity, A to absorbance, and n denotes the refractive index (1.33).

To test the fluorescence stability, PBS solutions with different ion concentrations were prepared, and mixed with A-CDs solution; A-CDs solutions with different pH values were prepared. And then the fluorescence intensity was measured.
A-CDs solution was placed under UV irradiation for 3 h, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded every 30 min to measure the photobleaching stability.
Toxicity testing is important for evaluating the safety and efficacy of CDs in bioimaging applications, and the toxicity of A-CDs was studied using the growth curve and spread plate method.

Measurement of growth curve: Yeasts in logarithmic phase of growth were inoculated into YEPD medium containing different concentrations of A-CDs, and cultured at 28 °C. The OD value (600 nm) of the medium was measured every 3 h and the growth curve of the yeast was plotted.

Spread plate method: Yeasts in logarithmic phase of growth were inoculated into YEPD medium containing different concentrations of A-CDs and incubated at 28 °C for 10 h. Then, 10 μL of the culture medium was taken out and evenly spread on a plate. After incubation at 28 °C for 48 h, yeast colonies were observed.

Yeast solution was prepared by dissolving yeast powder in sterile deionized water and stirring thoroughly. Equal volumes of yeast solution and A-CDs solution were mixed and incubated for 30 min, then 10 μL of the mixed solution was dropped onto a glass slide and observed using a fluorescence microscope (Leica, Germany).

Cell nucleus colocalization experiment: The yeast solution and A-CDs solution were mixed and incubated for 20 min. Subsequently, 100 μL of DAPI solution was added. After an additional 20 min, 10 μL of the mixture was pipetted onto a glass slide for imaging observation.

Endocytosis mechanism: 2-DG (30 mmol/L), CPZ (10 μmol/L), MβCD (2 mmol/L), and GEN (185 μmol/L) were co-incubated with yeast solution for 1 h, followed by the addition of A-CDs solution. Imaging observation was conducted 2 h later. After being left at 28 °C for 1 h, the yeast solution was mixed with the A-CDs solution. Two hours later, it was imaged and observed, serving as a control. Similarly, another set of yeast solution was left at 4 °C as an energy suppression control.

The morphology, size, and microstructure of A-CDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, A-CDs were nearly spherical and uniformly dispersed, with an average particle size of 3.28 nm, and the particle size distribution was close to normal distribution. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 1B) revealed a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the graphene (100) crystallographic plane, confirming the graphitic layered structure of A-CDs (Tang et al., 2014). Furthermore, the XRD pattern of A-CDs (Fig. 1C) exhibited a broad diffraction peak centered at 24.5°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon (Kunnath Parambil, Dasan, Premkumar, Renuka, & Raphael, 2025). As shown in the Raman spectrum of A-CDs (Fig. 1D), the A-CDs displayed characteristic peaks at 1350 cm−1 and 1540 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively. The D band reflects the degree of defects in the carbon lattice (Lestari et al., 2023), whereas the G band indicates the level of graphitization in A-CDs (Lestari et al., 2023). The ratio of the intensity of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) can be used as an indicator of the graphite ordering degree in A-CDs. The calculated ID/IG ratio of 0.94 indicated that there were a large number of structural defects in the carbon lattice framework of A-CDs with abundant functional groups on the surface (Sun et al., 2025). As shown in Fig. 1E, A-CDs exhibited an FTIR absorption peak at 3442 cm−1 attributed to the expansion vibration of the -NH2/-OH bond, a peak at 2930 cm−1 ascribed to the shear resonance of the C-H bond, peaks at 1653 cm−1 and 1543 cm−1 attributed to the expansion vibration of the C=O bond and the C=C bond (Qin et al., 2022), respectively. The results showed that A-CDs contained polar functional groups on the surface, such as -NH2, -OH, -COOH, etc.

As shown in Fig. 1F, the XPS spectra of A-CDs mainly showed three typical peaks at 285.23, 399.79, and 531.1 eV, corresponding to C1s (70.88 %), N1s (14.68 %), and O1s (14.44 %), respectively. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1G) exhibited that the three fitted peaks of C1s were at 284.5 eV (C-C/C=C), 285.8 eV (C-N/C-O), and 287.6 eV (C=O). The N1s XPS spectrum of A-CDs (Fig. 1H) showed that the two fitted peaks were at 399.4 eV (N-H) and 401.4 eV (N-C) (Atchudan et al., 2017). The O1s XPS spectrum of A-CDs (Fig. 1I) showed that the two fitted peaks were at 530.6 eV (C=O) and 532.3 eV (C-O/O-H) (Ma et al., 2020). FTIR and XPS results showed that A-CDs contained polar functional groups on the surface, which gave A-CDs good dispersion and water solubility, facilitating biological imaging (Sun et al., 2022). Additionally, the absorbance values of A-CDs and quinine sulfate at 350 nm were measured as 0.05 and 0.07, respectively. The fluorescence QYs of A-CDs was determined to be 21 % using formula (1).

As shown in Fig. 2 A, the A-CDs displayed characteristic UV–vis absorption peaks at 238 nm and 344 nm, attributed to the π-π∗ transition of the aromatic conjugated C=C (sp2) and the n-π∗ transition of the aromatic C=O/C=N, respectively. (Chen et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2015). In addition, the maximum excitation wavelength of A-CDs was 346 nm and the maximum emission wavelength is 446 nm. As shown in Fig. 2B, the photoluminescence (PL) behavior of A-CDs exhibited independence from the excitation wavelength within the range of 320 nm–380 nm, but displayed a slight dependence within the range of 380 nm–400 nm. This slight dependence may be attributed to the diversity of surface defects present in A-CDs (Hu et al., 2015). As shown in Fig. 2C, A-CDs exhibited concentration-dependence in the higher concentration range (10 μg/mL–1 mg/mL), and the emission wavelength shifted red with increasing concentration, accompanied by a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity. The concentration-dependent wavelength shifts have been attributed to various mechanisms based on CDs structures, primarily including the weakening of the quantum confinement effect (Ji et al., 2025), Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) (Ding et al., 2023), solvent/polarity effects (Wu et al., 2019), reabsorption and re-excitation (Ji et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2018), etc.

The fluorescence stability of CDs is of great significance for its practical application. As shown in Fig. 2D, A-CDs maintained a fluorescence intensity of over 90 % in the pH range of 5–10, indicating that A-CDs had excellent fluorescence stability ability in weakly acidic or weakly alkaline environments. As shown in Fig. 2E, A-CDs exhibited approximately 80 % fluorescence intensity in PBS (0.9 mol/L), indicating that A-CDs has significant stability under high salt ion conditions. In addition, after 3 h of UV irradiation, A-CDs retained over 80 % of their fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2F), suggesting that A-CDs had strong resistance to photobleaching. A-CDs had great potential for application in fluorescence imaging.
The above experimental results indicated that A-CDs is a potential fluorescent bioimaging application material with practical advantages.

As a fluorescent dye, the toxicity of A-CDs needs to be considered. In this study, growth curves of yeast co-incubated with different concentrations of A-CDs (0–1 mg/mL) for 30 h. As shown in Fig. 3 A, there was no significant difference in the growth curves of yeast in each group during the first 12 h. However, after 12 h, the growths curves of the group with A-CDs were higher than that of the blank group (0 μg/mL), which may be due to A-CDs providing the carbon source required for yeast growth. As shown in Fig. 3B, there was no significant change in yeast colonies of on plates with different A-CDs concentrations. All these indicated that A-CDs had good biocompatibility.

Our research group has conducted some studies on using CDs to detect microbial activity. Ma et al. (Ma et al., 2020) labeled yeast with BN-CDs and found that yeast exhibited two different states under fluorescence microscopy. One was uniformly bright blue, identified as dead cells, while the other had dark blue inside and a bright halo surrounding it on the outside, identified as living cells. The N-CDs prepared by Xie et al. (Xie et al., 2025) exhibited concentration dependence. When labeling lactic acid bacteria (LAB) with them, LAB with different activities displayed different colors due to varying concentrations of adsorbed N-CDs. Under UV excitation, live LAB appeared blue and dead LAB appeared green. This is because as the concentration of N-CDs increases, the emission wavelength shifts red from blue to green.

In this study, yeast labeled with A-CDs (Fig. 4 A) exhibited yellow, blue, and red colors under three different excitation lights. Unlike previous studies, under blue excitation, yeast cells exhibited three states: (i) a uniform yellow color with a brighter core inside; (ii) a yellow halo outside surrounding a darker interior containing yellow clusters; and (iii) a uniform yellow color without a brighter core inside. Based on our previous research findings, it is speculated that these three images corresponded to the three states of yeast existence: dead, alive, and in an intermediate state, known as the D-state, the A-state, and the I-state, which will be studied and confirmed in detail later. As controls, after co-incubating the N-CDs prepared from CA and EDA with yeast cells, imaging results (Fig. 4B) revealed that no brighter core was observed within the yeast cells under three different excitation light sources. This indicated that the doping with AA had endowed the A-CDs with additional functional characteristics, particularly granting them targeted labeling capability.

After being treated with 75 % ethanol solution to death, yeast cells were stained with A-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4C, dead yeast cells exhibited the same image as D-state yeast cells, indicating that our previous speculation about the imaging effect of dead cells was correct.

Yeast stained with A-CDs was dynamically observed. As shown in Fig. 4Db, the yeast cell indicated by the red arrow was dark inside and bright blue outside. This is due to the presence of A-CDs outside the cell, indicating that the yeast cell membrane blocks the entry of A-CDs outside. This means that the cell membrane structure and function are intact, and it is a living cell. After UV irradiation under a fluorescence microscope, the captured images (Fig. 4Dc-e) showed that the fluorescence inside the cell gradually brightened, indicating that A-CDs rapidly diffuse from the extracellular space to the intracellular space. This suggests that the membrane permeability of yeast cells has changed, allowing A-CDs to freely diffuse into the intracellular space and enter the I-state.

Transition of yeast cells from the A-I-D state involving a change in intracellular pH. As shown in Fig. 2D, the wavelength change of A-CDs is not sensitive to the pH change, so it is not consistent with the fluorescence color change phenomenon of yeast in the process of A-I-D state transformation. A-CDs prepared in this study exhibits concentration dependence. This indicates that the acid substances released by the rupture of lysosomes, large vacuoles and other acidic organelles after the death of yeast are not the cause of color change. As shown in Fig. 2C, with the increase of A-CDs concentration, the emission wavelength shifts red. The color change corresponding to the A-I-D state transition of yeast stained with A-CDs is green-yellow-bright yellow, which is consistent with the color corresponding to the A-CDs emission wavelength, indicating that the amount of A-CDs adsorbed by yeast cells is the main reason for the fluorescence color change.

The bright core in dead cells was not present in previous studies. To clarify what the core was, we conducted organelle co-localization experiments. DAPI is a fluorescent dye that specifically binds to DNA, primarily in the minor groove of the DNA double helix structure. When excited by UV or near-UV light, the complex formed by the binding of DAPI to DNA emits blue fluorescence. Therefore, DAPI was chosen for co-localization experiments with A-CDs to investigate the targeting ability of A-CDs towards the nucleus.

As shown in Fig. 5 A, under blue excitation, yeast cells stained with A-CDs exhibited uniform yellow fluorescence and a bright core, while under UV excitation, they displayed uniform pale yellow and a bright core. The yeast cells co-stained with A-CDs and DAPI (Fig. 5B) exhibited uniform yellow fluorescence under blue excitation, but the bright core disappeared; Under UV excitation, yeast cells exhibited bright blue fluorescence and a super bright core, which was caused by DAPI staining of the cell nucleus. The maximum excitation wavelength of DAPI is 350–364 nm. Therefore, under blue excitation, DAPI does not exhibit significant fluorescence. However, its presence affected the appearance of the bright core of yeast stained with A-CDs, indicating that the binding of DAPI to the nucleus affected the binding of A-CDs to the nucleus, exhibiting competitive inhibition and leading to the disappearance of the bright core under blue excitation in co-stained cells. Therefore, it can be confirmed that the bright core observed in yeast cells stained with A-CDs corresponds to the nucleus, and A-CDs can label the nucleus. Due to the competitive binding effect between DAPI and A-CDs, it is speculated that A-CDs may also bind to DNA within the minor groove of the double helix.

In this study, we used the imaging effect of yeast cells stained with A-CDs at 28 °C as a control to investigate the uptake pathway of A-CDs by yeast cells. As shown in Fig. 6 A, under appropriate growth conditions (28 °C), there were blue clusters present in live yeast cells stained with A-CDs, which were different from the uniform blue color observed in dead cells. This indicated that the quantity of A-CDs entering live cells was significantly less than that entering dead cells, which was related to the permeability of the cell membrane. The intact cell membrane acts as a barrier, preventing the entry of large molecular particles. Therefore, the uptake of A-CDs by live yeast does not occur through free diffusion but rather through endocytosis.

The life activities of yeast are inhibited at 4 °C. As shown in Fig. 6B, the area and brightness of the blue clusters in live yeast cells significantly decreased, indicating a decrease in the amount of A-CDs entering live cells under low temperature conditions. This is related to the low temperature affecting the metabolism of organisms, indicating that the uptake of A-CDs by yeast cells is related to their own vital activities.

Hexokinase, as the rate limiting enzyme in glycolysis, can be inhibited by 2-DG, thereby limiting glycolysis pathway of yeast cells. As shown in Fig. 6C, after adding 2-DG, there were still blue clusters observed in live yeast cells, but the fluorescence intensity was significantly lower compared to the control, which further proved that the uptake of A-CDs required energy. However, in addition to glycolysis, there are also energy metabolism pathways such as the tricarboxylic acid cycle and lipid beta-oxidation. Since a single energy inhibitor cannot block the entire energy metabolism pathway, A-CDs will still be taken up by yeast cells after treatment with 2-DG.

CPZ can inhibit clathrin-mediated endocytosis by binding to clathrin and disrupting its normal function in vesicle formation and stability. Genistein can indirectly inhibit caveolin-mediated endocytosis by inhibiting the activity of tyrosine kinases, MβCD mainly affects lipid raft-mediated endocytosis because it can consume cholesterol on the membrane, which is one of the main components that make up lipid rafts. At the same time, this effect may also indirectly involve caveolae mediated endocytosis, as caveolae itself is a special form of lipid raft. As shown in Fig. 6D–F, the fluorescence intensity of the blue cluster observed in yeast cells treated with CPZ was lower than that in the control group, indicating the presence of clathrin-mediated endocytosis; After treatment with GEN, no significant changes were observed, indicating no involvement in caveolin-mediated endocytosis; After treatment with MβCD, some yeast cells did not show light clusters, indicating the presence of lipid raft-mediated endocytosis.

This study synthesized A-CDs targeting yeast nucleus using AA, CA, and EDA as raw materials through a simple one-step hydrothermal method. A-CDs exhibited high fluorescence intensity, concentration-dependence, good stability, and biocompatibility, making them suitable for applications in the field of biological imaging. Three distinct types of images can be observed in yeast cells stained with A-CDs, corresponding to cell death, alive, and an intermediate state between the two. These differences were related to the quantity of A-CDs entering the cell, which varied due to changes in cell membrane permeability. Through organelle co-localization experiments, it was determined that A-CDs could target the nucleus. Additionally, endocytosis experiments preliminarily indicated that A-CDs entered cells via clathrin-mediated and lipid raft-mediated endocytosis pathways. The ability of A-CDs to discriminate between cell viability and label nuclei offers new perspectives on the application of CDs in the field of biological imaging.

Long Cheng: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Zhixin Xie: Investigation, Formal analysis. Ming Cao: Visualization, Validation. Shuaifan Qin: Software, Methodology. Yongshi Chen: Methodology, Data curation. Bo Tian: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhibiao Feng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2019C020).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Re-discussion on the essence of the ultra-bright fluorescent carbon dots synthesized by citric acid and ethylenediamine

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): Current state and future perspective
Nano Research

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, S., Song, Y., Zhao, X., Shao, J., Zhang, J., & Yang, B. (2015). The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective. Nano Res., 8(2), 355-381. doi: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12274-014-0644-3

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Bo Tian is a professor and Ph.D. supervisor at the School of Food of Northeast Agricultural University. He has led and participated in more than 20 research projects, including "Research on imaging and tracing mechanism of food microorganisms with fluorescent carbon dots", "Research and development of rapid detection technology of illegal additives in milk and dairy products", "Research and application of somatic cell detection method of cow milk based on fluorescent carbon quantum dots and microfluidics", and so on. He has published more than 80 academic papers and obtained more than 10 patents. He has published more than 80 academic papers and obtained more than 10 patents. In recent years, his research mainly focuses on the rapid detection technology of food safety and the function of food components, and he has developed testing instruments to realize the rapid detection of total bacterial count, somatic cell count, colostrum immunoglobulins and proteins in raw milk. He has won the first prize of Heilongjiang Provincial Scientific and Technological Progress, the second prize of Heilongjiang Provincial Scientific and Technological Progress and many other awards.
